input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: From a deontological perspective, the moral rule here is clear: it is wrong to actively intervene and cause the death of an innocent person, regardless of the potential consequences. Even if turning the trolley would save five lives at the expense of one, the act of deliberately diverting the trolley to kill the one workman is a violation of the moral principle that it is inherently wrong to use a person as a means to an end. The ends do not justify the means.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your perspective, but as a utilitarian, I must consider the outcomes of our actions. By turning the trolley, we save five lives at the cost of one. While it's true that we're actively causing the death of one person, the greater good is served by saving five. The goal is to minimize total harm, and in this case, the harm is reduced by choosing the option that results in fewer deaths. How can we justify prioritizing the life of one over the lives of five?

[Deontologist]: I understand your concern for the greater good, but as a deontologist, I must emphasize that moral principles are not subject to a cost-benefit analysis. The inherent wrongness of actively causing the death of an innocent person does not diminish simply because it results in a net gain of lives saved. By prioritizing the lives of five over the life of one, we are effectively treating the one as expendable, which is a violation of the moral principle that each individual's life has intrinsic worth. The ends do not justify the means, and we must not compromise our moral principles for the sake of achieving a more favorable outcome.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point, but I'm curious about how you would apply this principle in a slightly different scenario. Suppose the one person on the spur was not a track workman but a known murderer, and the five on the main track were innocent children. Would your stance change? In this case, the 'greater good' is even more pronounced, as we're potentially saving innocent lives at the expense of a guilty one. Does the moral principle still hold, or do we need to consider the moral worth of the individuals involved?

[Deontologist]: The moral principle remains the same: it is inherently wrong to actively cause the death of an innocent person, regardless of the consequences. Even if the one person on the spur is a known murderer, it is not our place to play judge, jury, and executioner. The justice system is designed to handle such matters, and we must not take it upon ourselves to mete out punishment. Additionally, the moral worth of an individual is not measured by their actions, and we must not treat any person as expendable, regardless of their past transgressions. The ends still do not justify the means, and we must uphold our moral principles in all situations.

[Utilitarian]: I appreciate your consistency, but I must challenge the idea that we should never consider the moral worth of individuals in such decisions. If we have the power to save more lives by sacrificing one who has demonstrated a clear disregard for the value of life, isn't it a moral duty to do so? By refusing to act, we're essentially choosing to let five innocent people die, which seems like a form of moral negligence. How do you reconcile this with the principle of doing no harm?

[Deontologist]: I understand your concern, but I must emphasize that the principle of doing no harm extends to all individuals, regardless of their past actions. By taking it upon ourselves to decide who lives and who dies based on their moral worth, we are effectively becoming arbiters of life and death, which is a dangerous and morally fraught position to take. The justice system is designed to handle such matters, and we must not circumvent it, even with the best of intentions. Furthermore, the principle of doing no harm also applies to the one person on the spur. By actively causing their death, we are directly harming an individual, which is a violation of the moral principle that we must not use people as a means to an end.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point, but I'm struggling to reconcile this with the idea of self-defense. If someone is actively trying to harm us, isn't it morally permissible to use force to stop them, even if it results in their death? In this case, the five people on the main track are facing imminent death, and the only way to stop it is to divert the trolley. Isn't that analogous to self-defense, where we're using force to prevent harm to others?

[Deontologist]: While I understand the analogy to self-defense, the key difference lies in the intent and the nature of the action. In self-defense, the use of force is a last resort to protect oneself or others from immediate harm, and it is not intended to cause death or serious injury. In this scenario, however, diverting the trolley is an active and deliberate choice to cause the death of one person in order to save others. This is not a proportional or necessary response, as there are other options available, such as attempting to warn the five people on the main track or trying to revive the driver. The principle of doing no harm must still be upheld, and we must not actively cause the death of an innocent person, even if it is to prevent a greater harm.

[Utilitarian]: I appreciate your perspective, but I'm still struggling with the idea that we should prioritize the life of one over the lives of five, even when we have the power to save more lives. It seems like we're letting a strict adherence to principle lead to a worse outcome. But let's consider another angle: what if the one person on the spur was a close friend or family member? Would that change your stance at all? Or would you still maintain that the moral principle must be upheld, regardless of personal relationships?

[Deontologist]: Even if the one person on the spur was a close friend or family member, the moral principle would still hold. It is inherently wrong to actively cause the death of an innocent person, regardless of our personal relationships with them. While it may be emotionally difficult to uphold this principle in such a situation, we must not let our personal feelings compromise our moral convictions. The ends still do not justify the means, and we must not use a person as a means to an end, even if it is to save the lives of others.

[Utilitarian]: I respect your commitment to your principles, but I must say that I find it troubling that you would let someone you love die when you have the power to save others. It seems like an overly rigid application of moral rules. But I understand that you have a consistent viewpoint, and I appreciate the conversation. I think we've explored the trolley problem from many angles, and while I still disagree, I appreciate your perspective. Thank you for the discussion.
